name: Platform-neutral access governance and procurement reform
consequences: Immediate: Mandating platform-neutral specifications prompts a rework of AltID procurement contracts and regulatory documentation. → Systemic: Platform-dependency index falls 30%, as non-Apple/Google signals gain prominence; procurement lead times increase by ~2 months and upfront compliance costs rise ~5%. → Strategic: Strengthens Danish digital sovereignty and resilience against vendor lock-in, but trades faster near-term delivery for higher upfront costs and longer negotiation cycles.
options: Institutionalize a platform-neutrality clause in AltID procurement specifications, with a staged sunset on Apple/Google trust anchors.
Mandate a dual-path procurement approach that requires both open-standard fallback and current app-store channels, with defined milestones.
Create a cross-sector platform-neutral identity exchange powered by self-sovereign identity and open-source PKI, governed by a multi-stakeholder consortium.
review: Controls centralization vs. platform neutrality. Weakness: The options fail to consider transition costs and coordination overhead across agencies.

name: Fallback authentication demonstrators under open standards
consequences: Immediate: Demonstrators validate a fallback path using WebAuthn/OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS and hardware tokens. → Systemic: Platform-dependency in test environment declines by 25%, while integration and maintenance costs rise 12%. → Strategic: Demonstrates viability of platform-neutral approaches to resilience, but requires ongoing funding and governance.
options: Develop non-production demonstrators validating a fallback path using WebAuthn on GrapheneOS and hardware tokens.
Extend the demonstrator to browser-based fallback with portable attestations and a two-domain testing scenario across at least two ministries.
Prototype a decentralized fallback architecture using SSI-based attestations and token-based governance to coordinate cross-minister identity flows.
review: Controls experimentation vs. platform reliability. Weakness: The options fail to consider the scalability of open-standards in live environments.

name: EU-aligned interoperability and standards push
consequences: Immediate: Initiates EU-aligned interoperability work and procurement coordination. → Systemic: Interoperability readiness improves by 20%, but cross-border coordination adds 1–2 months to schedules and 7% higher integration costs. → Strategic: Denmark gains influence in EU wallet policy, reducing future friction but accepting longer timelines.
options: Map Danish AltID requirements to EU wallet standards and embed interoperability criteria in procurement specs.
Participate in EU NSIS wallet groups, publish white papers, and align with eIDAS2 for fallback flows.
Lead a cross-border interoperability sandbox with multi-country pilots aligning with the European Digital Identity Wallet to harden platform-neutral flows.
review: Controls national sovereignty vs. EU interoperability. Weakness: The options fail to consider misalignment risks of cross-border governance and schedule delays.

name: Resilience-oriented coalition and policy advocacy
consequences: Immediate: Coalition-building improves political support for platform-neutrality. → Systemic: Policy acceptance rises by 15%, but budgetary friction creates 3–6 month procurement delays. → Strategic: Increases legitimacy and sustainability of the effort but may slow execution due to fiscal constraints.
options: Form a formal resilience coalition spanning civil society, privacy advocates, and academia, and publish a policy brief advocating platform neutrality.
Engage Folketinget's digital policy committee with quarterly roundtables and a commitment to a neutrality roadmap tied to budget cycles.
Build a dynamic governance cluster that co-funds ongoing platform-neutral piloting and procurement reform with shared risk.
review: Controls policy leadership vs. stakeholder fragmentation. Weakness: The options fail to consider budgetary constraints and procurement cycle friction.

name: Risk-sharing procurement and platform-neutrality funding models
consequences: Immediate: Procurement terms embed platform-neutrality incentives. → Systemic: Delivery probability improves by 25% when milestones are met; administration and contract-management costs rise ~8%. → Strategic: Momentum toward neutrality is strengthened, but governance complexity and cost of contract oversight increase.
options: Embed milestone-based risk-sharing contracts to incentivize platform-neutral readiness.
Introduce shared-savings procurement models with co-funding and joint pilots.
Launch a 'platform-neutrality as a service' funding model with performance-based contracts and vendor-provided fallback infrastructure.
review: Controls risk-transfer vs. cost of governance. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term maintenance costs and vendor lock-in risk if metrics are poorly defined.

name: Self-sovereign identity and decentralized fallback exploration
consequences: Immediate: Legal and governance scaffolding for SSI are established; initial prototypes are tested. → Systemic: Platform-dependency on centralized OS signals declines by 40%, but governance risk rises 15%. → Strategic: Long-term sovereignty path established; however regulatory complexity and risk of fragmentation may slow adoption.
options: Draft regulatory groundwork to enable SSI-based fallback with privacy guardrails.
Create a sandboxed SSI prototype with cross-border attestations and a governance framework.
Launch an open, token-governed SSI ecosystem with distributed rekeying and citizen-led identity attestation for public services.
review: Controls centralized trust signals vs. decentralization. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory clarity and data governance issues in SSI.

name: Platform-Neutral Trust Anchors Strategy
consequences: Immediate: A governance baseline is established and two ministries initiate pilots on platform-neutral trust anchors → Systemic: Vendor-dependency risk reduces by 25% and cross-provider onboarding time improves by 15%, but upfront coordination costs rise by 9% → Strategic: The project strengthens resilience and sovereignty, yet procurement cycles may lengthen and require sustained cross-government governance.
options: Formalize a governance framework to codify platform-neutral trust anchors and pilot it within two ministries.
Integrate a multi-provider attestation ecosystem while maintaining centralized oversight and standardization.
Adopt distributed identifiers (DIDs) and verifiable credentials as a radical platform-neutral trust anchor, backed by a public-private funding model and open-source implementation.
review: Controls platform-dependency vs regulatory sovereignty. Weakness: The options fail to consider transition costs and procurement-cycle timing.

name: Open-Standards Credential Exchange Roadmap
consequences: Immediate: Execute a standards-based credential exchange pilot across two public portals → Systemic: Interoperability across the pilot increases by 20% and onboarding time for new providers decreases by 15%, but certification overhead grows by 10% → Strategic: The ecosystem becomes more globally compatible and scalable, but the added certification overhead may slow near-term procurement.
options: Codify a staged protocol upgrade that adopts existing open standards (FIDO2, OpenID Connect) with cross-border pilots in government portals.
Establish a formal multi-provider credential exchange layer using open standards with shared attestation and sandboxed interoperability testing.
Launch a radical cross-domain wallet network using verifiable credentials anchored in a blockchain-backed revocation registry, financed by a value-sharing procurement model.
review: Controls open standards adoption speed vs. integration complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider certification overhead and cross-border alignment.

name: Procurement Diversification and Resilience Pilot
consequences: Immediate: Implement a vendor-diversification framework into future procurements with risk scoring and alternative-provider contingencies → Systemic: Diversification reduces single-vendor risk by 25% and improves resilience, but initial contract-management overhead increases by 12% → Strategic: The project secures continuity and cost competitiveness, but admin burden and negotiation timelines may lengthen.
options: Introduce a vendor-diversification framework in future procurements with risk scoring and mandatory alternative-provider contingencies.
Pilot multi-supplier contracts with clear SLAs for continuity and transparent performance metrics.
Create a regional identity services cooperative funded by shared capital, offering community-owned services and competitive pricing.
review: Controls supplier diversification risk vs procurement efficiency. Weakness: The options fail to consider budgetary constraints and long-term diversification costs.

name: Fallback-Ready AltID Interface Standards
consequences: Immediate: Define a universal fallback authentication interface and begin AltID readiness checks across public portals → Systemic: Adoption reduces platform-dependency risk by 20% and improves fallback time by 25%, but hardware costs for tokens and training add 8% to the budget → Strategic: The plan strengthens resilience and continuity, but user experience and operational complexity increase.
options: Define a universal fallback authentication interface and integrate AltID readiness checks across public portals.
Develop a plug-and-play fallback path using hardware tokens and WebAuthn with formal certification and vendor-agnostic APIs.
Implement a radical offline-first fallback using secure hardware modules with offline attestation and distributed revocation.
review: Controls platform accessibility vs user friction. Weakness: The options fail to consider hardware costs and user training requirements.

name: Continuity-Driven Regulatory Alignment Plan
consequences: Immediate: Initiate regulatory alignment activities to clarify platform-neutral fallback requirements → Systemic: Regulatory clarity reduces compliance risk by 18% and accelerates procurement readiness by 12%, but lobbying and policy negotiation costs rise by 7% → Strategic: The project gains governance support and long-term stability, but advocacy burden and policy trade-offs require sustained commitment.
options: Lobby for regulatory amendments to clarify platform-neutral fallback requirements and procurement latitude.
Draft EU-compliant fallback specifications and NSIS standards for continuity in national procurement.
Establish a regulatory sandbox to experiment with platform-neutral fallback solutions and publish results.
review: Controls regulatory rigidity vs innovation pace. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-border enforcement complexities.

name: EU Identity Wallet Collaboration Push
consequences: Immediate: Join EU working groups to align Danish standards with wallet frameworks → Systemic: Denmark gains access to EU wallet interoperability benefits, boosting scalability by 15% and reducing duplication costs by 10%, but alignment costs and political risk rise by 6% → Strategic: The project gains EU legitimacy and scale, but national sovereignty concerns and governance overhead increase.
options: Join EU working groups to align Danish standards with wallet frameworks and contribute to shared specs.
Co-develop a national common interface spec with EU wallet pilots and run joint pilots.
Negotiate a pan-European procurement and funding alliance to build a platform-neutral fallback wallet, financed through cross-border grants.
review: Controls national sovereignty vs EU-wide interoperability. Weakness: The options fail to consider enforcement of cross-border rules and funding alignment.

name: Platform-Independent Access Governance for Danish Public Services
consequences: Immediate: Establish baseline governance for platform-neutral access across essential public services → Systemic: Reduces vendor concentration by 25% (vendor-concentration index) but increases cross-agency coordination overhead and procurement cycle times by 12% → Strategic: Positions Denmark to withstand platform policy shocks and advance digital sovereignty, but requires sustained funding and formal coalition management. Capacity-building across ministries will be required to manage governance overhead. The balance between resilience and administrative burden will shape timeline and cost.
options: Consolidate cross-government platform-neutral access requirements into procurement language and pilot across two ministries.
Scale a formal fallback authentication path using open standards across all critical portals while preserving MitID compatibility.
Establish a distributed fallback identity network using verifiable credentials anchored on a public blockchain with tokenized governance and shared risk pools.
review: Controls centralization vs local autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider transition costs.

name: Open-Standards Fallback Credential Experimentation
consequences: Immediate: Initiate small-scale demonstrations of fallback credentials built on open standards across two ministries → Systemic: Reduces vendor lock-in by 15% but increases interoperability testing burden and security review requirements by 18% due to multi-vendor integration → Strategic: Builds credibility for platform-neutral solutions but slows policy streamlining and requires ongoing stakeholder engagement. Capacity planning must reflect the expanded test program.
options: Codify a two-vendor fallback path with open standards and pilot in two agencies.
Pilot a browser-based or hardware-token fallback authenticator to reduce reliance on the mobile OS duopoly.
Create a decentralized identity network using verifiable credentials anchored on a public blockchain with tokenized governance.
review: Controls centralization vs local autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider transition costs.

name: Procurement Architecture for Neutrality and Resilience
consequences: Immediate: Embed platform-neutral fallback requirements into a government-wide procurement playbook and demand multi-vendor demos across at least two agencies → Systemic: Broadens supplier participation by 30% but lengthens procurement cycles by 15% due to heightened evaluation complexity → Strategic: Expands resilience and sovereignty, but raises admin costs and necessitates clear milestone targets.
options: Codify platform-neutral fallback requirements in a government-wide procurement playbook and require multi-vendor demos.
Incentivize broad vendor participation through performance-based funding and open-source components.
Adopt a hybrid procurement model enabling a marketplace of platform-agnostic identity vendors and a public fallback service with EU NSIS alignment.
review: Controls centralization vs local autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider transition costs.

name: AltID Standards Influence and EU Policy Alignment
consequences: Immediate: Seek Danish adoption of EU-aligned AltID standards and NSIS-oriented procurement language → Systemic: Improves cross-border interoperability by ~20% but raises alignment costs and governance overhead by ~10% across agencies → Strategic: Enables faster EU wallet integration while sustaining lobbying and regulatory engagement.
options: Pursue EU-level mandate for platform-neutrality in AltID procurement language and standards.
Lead a Danish-EU standards profile with NSIS and European Digital Identity Wallet.
Develop a cross-border identity infrastructure using blockchain-anchored attestations and token-based governance across EU member states.
review: Controls centralization vs local autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider transition costs.

name: Resilience-Driven Public-Private Coalition Financing
consequences: Immediate: Establish a cross-sector funding framework with milestone-linked disbursements and independent audits → Systemic: Elevates resilience investment by 25% of budget through co-financing but increases complexity of reporting and risk-sharing governance by 15% → Strategic: Secures long-term funding for platform-neutral efforts but raises costs and accountability demands.
options: Create a formal cross-government-private financing arrangement with milestones and reporting.
Leverage grant-tranches and third-party audits.
Launch a tokenized risk-sharing fund with a blockchain-governed governance model.
review: Controls centralization vs local autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider transition costs.

name: Multi-Platform Trust Signals and Certification Scheme
consequences: Immediate: Launch a cross-platform trust-signal certification program for identity-agnostic authentication → Systemic: Increases trust-signal interoperability by 30% across platforms but raises certification costs and testing time by 18% → Strategic: Improves resilience and user confidence but requires ongoing maintenance and external assurance; privacy compliance burden escalates.
options: Establish a cross-platform trust signals certification for platform-agnostic authentication.
Run a phased certification rollout that includes open-source components and external security reviews.
Construct a blockchain-backed attestation network and AI-driven credential verification to certify trust signals across platforms.
review: Controls centralization vs local autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider transition costs.
